metadata.summary: Nygard ADRs document architectural decisions co-located with code: context, decision, alternatives considered with rejection reasons, and consequences (positive, negative, neutral).

content.title: Architecture Decision Records (ADR)
content.summary: A single-file document (Nygard format) that captures one architectural decision — its context, the decision made, alternatives considered with rejection reasons, and consequences (positive, negative, neutral). ADRs live in docs/adr/NNN-title.md, are numbered sequentially from 001, and transition through statuses: Proposed → Accepted → Deprecated/Superseded. New ADRs amend, never edit, existing Accepted ones.

why.p: Architecture decisions made today are invisible to new team members and to agents working months later. Without ADRs, the same debates recur; production incidents lack design context; and agents generating new code may contradict existing architectural choices they cannot see. Storing ADRs co-located with code in version control gives agents file-system access to the full decision history during code generation and review.

list.item: Before committing to a tech stack choice that will be hard to reverse (database, framework, auth strategy).
After a production incident that exposes a design weakness — capture the fix decision.
When a new team member keeps asking "why did we choose X?" — ADR is the answer.
Anytime two or more viable alternatives were seriously considered.
At the start of a new feature with meaningful architectural surface (new service, new API contract, new data model).
Trivial implementation details (which library function to call, naming conventions).
Decisions that will certainly be revisited within 1-2 weeks — too early, no context yet.
Configuration values that belong in docs, not decision records.
When there was only one realistic option — no choice means no ADR needed.

section.name: nygard-format

rule.statement: Every ADR must contain five sections: Context (situation and forces), Decision (active voice "We will..."), Alternatives Considered (at least 2 with pros, cons, and rejection reason), Consequences (positive, negative, neutral), Related Decisions.
rule.rationale: Missing the Alternatives section is the most common quality failure. Without alternatives, the ADR does not explain why other options were rejected — new team members will re-open the decision without knowing what was already considered.

section.reference: Nygard ADR skeleton with all required sections and field descriptions.

rule.statement: All new ADRs are written with status "Proposed". A human must change it to "Accepted" before the ADR is considered active. Agents must not self-approve ADRs.
rule.rationale: "Proposed" is a human-in-loop gate. Self-approving skips the review step where affected teams can object or add constraints. An ADR that bypasses review may conflict with decisions in other parts of the system.

rule.statement: When superseding an existing ADR, create a new ADR and update the old one's status to "Superseded by ADR-NNN". Never edit an Accepted ADR's content — immutability is the convention.
rule.rationale: Editing Accepted ADRs destroys the historical record. The original decision context is valuable even after the decision is superseded — it explains why the earlier choice made sense at the time.

list.item: Proposed: under discussion, gathering feedback.
Accepted: approved, active decision.
Deprecated: no longer relevant, kept for history.
Superseded: replaced by a newer ADR (link required).

rule.statement: Write in active voice: "We will use PostgreSQL" not "PostgreSQL was chosen". Keep each ADR under 400 words. If it is longer, the decision scope is too broad — split it.
rule.rationale: Passive voice obscures agency and makes the decision seem inevitable rather than chosen. Length over 400 words typically means the ADR contains implementation details that belong in design.md.

rule.statement: Store ADRs in docs/adr/NNN-title.md in the same repository as the code. Number sequentially from 001. Never reuse a number even if an ADR is deleted.
rule.rationale: Co-location with code gives agents file-system access to ADRs during code generation and review. Sequential numbering creates a stable citation scheme (AD-X references in design.md and tasks).

rule.statement: Before writing an ADR, the agent must read docs/adr/ to find the next sequential number, then read the relevant feature spec.md and design.md to source Context, Decision, and Consequences. Do not invent consequences not grounded in these documents.
rule.rationale: Invented consequences are the second most common quality failure after missing alternatives. Consequences must be traceable to real design tradeoffs, not generic technology wisdom.

rule.statement: After writing, set status to "Proposed" and stop. The agent must not proceed to Accepted without human review.
rule.rationale: Human sign-off is the primary quality gate for ADRs. Agents that self-approve skip the review where affected teams identify conflicts with other architectural constraints.

section.name: adr-compliance-review
section.p: To audit whether accepted ADRs are still being followed, an agent reads docs/adr/, then inspects the codebase for each Accepted decision. Output: table of ADR number, status, "still followed?" (yes/no), and notes. Flag any violation as "review-needed".

rule.statement: Run ADR compliance review at major version bumps and after significant refactors. Mark obsolete decisions as "Deprecated" — do not leave orphaned Accepted ADRs that no longer reflect the codebase.
rule.rationale: Orphaned Accepted ADRs mislead agents and new team members. An ADR that says "We will use PostgreSQL" when the project now uses MongoDB is worse than no ADR.

antipattern.description: ADR used as a design guide: detailed implementation instructions in the Decision section.
antipattern.reason: ADRs document the choice, not the implementation. Implementation details belong in design.md. An ADR with implementation details will become stale as the implementation evolves.

antipattern.description: Missing Alternatives section or alternatives stated as "Option A and Option B were considered."
antipattern.reason: Without pros, cons, and rejection reasons per alternative, the ADR does not prevent re-opening the debate. Alternatives must include why each was rejected.

antipattern.description: ADRs stored only in a wiki (Confluence, Notion) separate from the codebase.
antipattern.reason: Agents cannot access wiki content via file system during code generation. Orphaned ADRs drift out of sync with the actual implementation.

antipattern.description: Agents fabricating alternatives in the Alternatives Considered section without grounding in real context.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated alternatives undermine the credibility of the entire ADR. Always supply spec.md and design.md as input when asking an agent to write alternatives.

antipattern.description: Consequences section copied from the Decision section with different wording.
antipattern.reason: Consequences must describe downstream effects, not restate the decision. Require distinct, actionable consequence bullets in the prompt.

antipattern.description: Passing all 50+ ADR files to an agent instead of just the index list.
antipattern.reason: Full ADR history consumes 20-40k tokens. Pass only the index (NNN | Title | Status) and load individual ADRs on demand when their content is needed.

template.purpose: Nygard ADR skeleton with all required sections and field descriptions.
Bash script: lists all ADRs in docs/adr/ with their status line for quick index review.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — reads ADRs as AD-X citations in design.md; enforces decisions during code generation.
General research subagent (claude-opus-4-7) — drafts alternatives section and consequences; requires broad context.
adr-tools: CLI to create, list, and link ADRs in standard format. Install: brew install adr-tools. Docs: https://github.com/npryce/adr-tools
log4brains: Markdown-based ADR knowledge base with web UI. Install: npx log4brains init. Docs: https://github.com/thomvaill/log4brains
pyadr: Python CLI for ADR lifecycle management. Install: pip install pyadr. Docs: https://github.com/opinionated-digital-center/pyadr
GitHub (docs/adr/ in repo): Best — ADRs versioned with code, agents can read via file system.
Confluence: ADRs stored in wiki; agents can write via API but content drifts from code.
Notion: Flexible; works if team uses Notion for docs; link to code PRs manually.
Backstage: ADR plugin available; good for large orgs with service catalogs.
log4brains: Static site generation from docs/adr/; zero API needed.

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.p: For writing ADRs from spec/design context:
section.code: Read docs/adr/ to find the next ADR number. Read {feature}/spec.md to extract decision AD-{N}. Write docs/adr/{NNN}-{slug}.md using the Nygard format. Status must be "Proposed". List at least 2 alternatives considered and why each was rejected. Do not invent consequences — base them only on information in spec.md and design.md.

Review existing ADRs in docs/adr/. For each ADR with status "Accepted", check if the codebase still follows the decision. Output a table: ADR number | Status | Still followed? | Notes. Flag any ADR that appears to be violated as "review-needed".

list.item: Number sequentially from 001; never reuse a number even if an ADR is deleted.
Write in active voice: "We will use PostgreSQL" not "PostgreSQL was chosen".
Keep each ADR under 400 words; if it is longer, the decision scope is too broad — split it.
Link related ADRs bidirectionally in the "Related Decisions" section.
Store in docs/adr/ in the same repository as the code; agents need file-system access.
When superseding, update the old ADR's status to "Superseded by ADR-NNN" — do not delete.
Review ADRs at major version bumps; mark obsolete ones as "Deprecated".
ADRs are immutable by convention; agents must create a new ADR to amend decisions, never edit an Accepted one.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common patterns when agents write or review ADRs:

list.item: Agents will fabricate alternatives if not grounded in real context — always provide spec.md and design.md as input.
"Proposed" status is a human-in-loop gate; agents must not skip it even when certain.
Agents writing ADRs tend to copy-paste consequences from the Decision section — require distinct, actionable consequence bullets in the prompt.
Long ADR histories (50+ files) increase context cost; pass only the index list to agents, not all ADR content.
ADR content is immutable by convention; agents must create a new ADR to amend decisions, never edit an Accepted one.
